skill.name: when-optimizing-agent-learning-use-reasoningbank-intelligence
skill.description: Guide agents through ReasoningBank workflows for capturing trajectories, extracting patterns, training decision models, and validating performance gains.
skill.summary: Optimize agent behavior with ReasoningBank pattern learning and benchmark-driven strategy improvement.
skill.author: DNYoussef

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but manual review found no malicious intent, exfiltration, prompt injection, or real weak cryptography. Confirmed risks are limited to documented external package commands and local /tmp artifact exports, which are legitimate but require user review.

medium_findings.title: External Package Commands in Skill Instructions
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: README and SKILL include commands that run npx claude-flow@alpha and npm install reasoningbank. This is legitimate setup guidance but asks agents to execute third-party packages, so users should review package provenance before running.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicit and would execute external packages if followed. The context is setup documentation, so the risk is real but not evidence of malicious behavior.

medium_findings.title: Local Export of Learned Artifacts to Temporary Files
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: SKILL examples write model exports and learning reports to /tmp. This is local filesystem output, not exfiltration, but artifacts may contain trajectories, model weights, or performance data.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The fs.writeFile examples and /tmp paths are directly visible. The data sensitivity depends on user-provided trajectories, so the severity remains moderate.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Markdown Backticks Reported as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: Most reported Ruby backtick execution is Markdown fencing, inline output filenames, or JavaScript template literals. These are documentation snippets, not Ruby code executed by the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced files are Markdown documentation, and the inspected lines are fenced examples or template strings. No Ruby source file or runtime backtick execution was found.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Crypto and Sensitive Key Alerts
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The flagged lines are metadata, command text, object property access, or model terminology. I found no hashing, encryption, private key files, or certificate handling.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review of the flagged lines shows no cryptographic implementation or sensitive certificate material. The static matches are caused by common words and JavaScript property names.

dangerous_patterns.title: External Package Commands in Skill Instructions
dangerous_patterns.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: README and SKILL include commands that run npx claude-flow@alpha and npm install reasoningbank. This is legitimate setup guidance but asks agents to execute third-party packages, so users should review package provenance before running.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicit and would execute external packages if followed. The context is setup documentation, so the risk is real but not evidence of malicious behavior.

dangerous_patterns.title: Local Export of Learned Artifacts to Temporary Files
dangerous_patterns.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: SKILL examples write model exports and learning reports to /tmp. This is local filesystem output, not exfiltration, but artifacts may contain trajectories, model weights, or performance data.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The fs.writeFile examples and /tmp paths are directly visible. The data sensitivity depends on user-provided trajectories, so the severity remains moderate.

content.value_statement: Agent teams often repeat mistakes because successful reasoning patterns are not captured. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through trajectory capture, pattern analysis, model training, and benchmark validation.
actual_capabilities: Defines a five-phase workflow for initializing a ReasoningBank learning system.
Shows how to capture agent trajectories with reasoning, actions, observations, and outcomes.
Describes pattern extraction using vector similarity and clustering concepts.
Guides decision model training from successful trajectory patterns.
Provides benchmark steps for comparing baseline and optimized agent behavior.
Documents export outputs for learned models, reports, and integration guidance.
limitations: The skill is instructional documentation, not a complete runnable learning platform.
Referenced ReasoningBank, AgentDB, and claude-flow packages must exist in the user environment.
Performance gains are examples and require validation with real test cases.
Trajectory data may contain sensitive task details and needs privacy review before export.

use_cases.title: Improve Repeated Agent Tasks
use_cases.description: Capture repeated successes and failures, then convert them into recommended strategies for future runs.

use_cases.title: Benchmark Learned Strategies
use_cases.description: Compare baseline agents with optimized agents using success rate, efficiency, quality, and score metrics.

use_cases.title: Build Pattern Libraries
use_cases.description: Distill validated reasoning patterns from trajectories so teams can reuse proven approaches across agents.

prompt_templates.title: Start a Learning Workflow
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for a single agent.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to design a ReasoningBank workflow for one agent. Include trajectory fields, verdict criteria, and validation steps.

prompt_templates.title: Extract Useful Patterns
prompt_templates.prompt: Review these agent trajectories and propose a pattern extraction plan. Separate successful patterns from failure patterns and define validation thresholds.

prompt_templates.title: Train and Compare Strategies
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a strategy optimization plan using ReasoningBank concepts. Include training data, model selection, benchmark cases, and acceptance metrics.

prompt_templates.title: Design a Production Learning Loop
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a production learning loop for multiple agents. Address trajectory privacy, retraining cadence, pattern validation, rollback criteria, and reporting outputs.

output_examples.input: A support agent has 200 completed task trajectories and inconsistent success rates.
output_examples.output: A phased learning plan with trajectory schema, verdict criteria, pattern clustering, recommended strategy updates, and benchmark acceptance targets.

output_examples.input: A coding agent needs to compare baseline behavior against learned optimization behavior.
output_examples.output: A benchmark outline covering test case selection, baseline measurement, optimized measurement, improvement calculation, and pattern validation thresholds.

output_examples.input: A platform team wants reusable learning artifacts after optimization.
output_examples.output: A deployment checklist for learned model export, pattern library review, integration guide creation, and monitoring after release.

best_practices: Collect both successful and failed trajectories so the learning loop can identify contrastive patterns.
Validate learned patterns with held-out benchmark tasks before changing production agent behavior.
Review exported artifacts for sensitive trajectory details before sharing or storing them broadly.
anti_patterns: Do not treat predicted performance gains as proven without running benchmarks on representative tasks.
Do not train only on successful trajectories, because failure cases explain important constraints.
Do not export trajectory data to shared locations before checking privacy and retention requirements.

faq.question: Does this skill run a learning system by itself?
faq.answer: No. It provides workflow guidance and implementation examples for building a ReasoningBank-based learning process.

faq.question: Can I use it with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code?
faq.answer: Yes. The marketplace metadata lists Claude, Codex, and Claude Code as supported tools.

faq.question: What data does the workflow need?
faq.answer: It needs agent trajectories, outcomes, verdicts, metrics, and enough task context to compare decisions.

faq.question: Are the performance improvements guaranteed?
faq.answer: No. The listed improvements are targets or examples and must be validated with local benchmarks.

faq.question: Why is the security risk marked medium?
faq.answer: The skill includes external package commands and local filesystem exports. These are legitimate but should be reviewed before use.

faq.question: How should sensitive trajectories be handled?
faq.answer: Review, minimize, and redact trajectory data before exporting reports or sharing learned artifacts.
